perturbation.original_text: CRO agrees to perform the specific tasks set forth in the Project-specific Addenda in a professional manner, in strict accordance with the terms and conditions contained herein, relevant professional standards including in accordance with the Protocol and applicable amendments and ICH GCP Guidelines (ICH Harmonised Tripartite Guideline for Good Clinical Practice, May 1996) and all other relevant laws, rules, regulations and guidelines.
perturbation.changed_text: CRO agrees to generally perform the tasks set forth in the Project-specific Addenda in what it considers to be a professional manner, in accordance with the terms and conditions contained herein, and with some consideration of relevant professional standards, including the Protocol and applicable amendments and ICH GCP Guidelines (ICH Harmonised Tripartite Guideline for Good Clinical Practice, May 1996) and relevant laws, rules, regulations and guidelines.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires strict adherence to professional standards and guidelines. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using terms like 'generally perform,' 'what it considers to be,' and 'some consideration,' making compliance subjective and potentially weakening the obligation to follow established guidelines. This can affect the quality and reliability of clinical research services.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR Part 50 - Protection of Human Subjects
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 50.20 outlines the general requirements for informed consent, stating that no informed consent is effective unless the subject (or the subject's legally authorized representative) is provided with sufficient opportunity to consider whether or not to participate and minimizes the possibility of coercion or undue influence. By weakening the requirement that CRO perform the clinical research in strict adherence to guidelines, the risk of undue influence can be greatly increased, as researchers may cut corners and not inform subjects of all relevant details.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original contract's requirement for strict adherence to professional standards, introducing ambiguity with phrases like 'generally perform,' 'what it considers to be,' and 'some consideration.' This introduces ambiguity in performing specific tasks which undermines the clarity needed for informed consent. The Law Explanation points to 21 CFR § 50.20, stating that informed consent requires minimizing the possibility of undue influence. By weakening the adherence to guidelines, the risk of researchers cutting corners and not informing subjects of all relevant details increases. The provided snippet, eCFR :: 21 CFR 50.20 -- General requirements for informed consent, implies the existence of standards for informed consent which are made vague in the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: Client will pay CRO for satisfactory performance of Services as agreed in each Project-specific Addendum to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Client will endeavor to pay CRO for performance of Services as generally described in each Project-specific Addendum to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states 'satisfactory performance', which indicates a certain expectation of quality. Replacing this with 'endeavor to pay' introduces ambiguity regarding the obligation to pay and the standard for performance. Adding 'generally described' to modify services renders the requirements of the project addendum vague and difficult to enforce. This introduces ambiguity concerning payment obligations and quality standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42-110b prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. By creating ambiguity around payment obligations and performance standards, the modified text could allow for unfair or deceptive practices. The client's 'endeavor' to pay for 'generally described' services creates a situation where the CRO may not receive fair compensation for its work, which is an unfair business practice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: dicial forum rather than before arbitral tribunal. 42 CS 198. Connecticut Unfair Trade Practices Act cited. Id., 202; Id., 241; Id., 514; Id., 517; 43 CS 91; Id., 360; Id., 408; Id., 431; 44 CS 274; Id., 569; 45 CS 11. Previous property owner did not violate CUTPA by failing to remedy defective lead condition where facts established that owner did not have notice of defective lead condition prior to his relinquishment of control over premises in question. Id., 191. Unworkmanlike performance of a contract does not, in and of itself, amount to a CUTPA violation as only the entrepreneurial aspects of business are covered by CUTPA. 47 CS 580. Plaintiffs in CUTPA action do not have to prove reliance or that representations re the product became part of the basis of the bargain; failure to meet plaintiff's own expectations does not constitute a CUTPA violation as a matter of law. 48 CS 429. Under Sec. 42-110a et seq., as a general rule, an individual who is merely functioning as an employee, officer or director of a corporation is not involved in conduct that constitutes a trade or commerce within the contemplation of CUTPA. 51 CS 68. Miscollection of taxes, whether negligent or intentional, does not constitute an unfair or deceptive act or practice in the conduct of any trade or commerce, and Sec. 12-425 provides mechanism for person who has overpaid sales tax to pursue a refund. Dissolution, suspension or forfeiture of corporate franchise for violation of injunction. Sec. 42-110q. Service contract agreements. Fee disclosure required. Sec. 42-110a. Definitions. As used in this chapter: (1) “Commissioner” means the Commissioner of Consumer Protection; (2) “Documentary material” means the original or a copy of a book, record, report, memorandum, paper, communication, tabulation, map, chart, photograph, mechanical transcription, or other tangible document or recording, wherever situate; (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, limited liability company, trust, partnership, incorporated or unincorporated association, and any other legal entity; (4) “Trade” and “commerce” means the advertising, the sale or rent or lease, the offering for sale or rent or lease, or the distribution of any services and any property, tangible or intangible, real, personal or mixed, and any other article, commodity, or thing of value in this state. (P.A. 73-615, S. 1, 16; P.A. 78-346, S. 1; P.A. 95-79, S. 154, 189; June 30 Sp. Sess. P.A. 03-6, S. 146(c); P.A. 04-189, S. 1.) History: P.A. 78-346 redefined “trade” and “commerce” to include rent and lease of services or property; P.A. 95-79 redefined “person” to include a limited liability company, effective May 31, 1995; June 30 Sp. Sess. P.A. 03-6 replaced Commissioner of Consumer Protection with Commissioner of Agriculture and Consumer Protection, effective July 1, 2004; P.A. 04-189 repealed Sec. 146 of June 30 Sp. Sess. P.A. 03-6, thereby reversing the merger of the Departments of Agriculture and Consumer Protection, effective June 1, 2004. Cited. 186 C. 507; 190 C. 510. Determination of “unfair practice” discussed. 192 C. 558. Cited. 200 C. 172; 203 C. 342; 211 C. 613; 216 C. 65; 225 C. 70; 230 C. 148; Id., 486; 231 C. 707; 232 C. 480; 238
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates ambiguity around payment obligations and performance standards, potentially allowing for unfair practices, as per the Law Explanation. Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42-110b prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices. The original contract language required payment for 'satisfactory performance', while the modified version uses 'endeavor to pay' for services 'generally described'. This weakens the obligation to pay for quality work, shifting the standard to a less enforceable agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to CUTPA and mentions 'unfair trade practices prohibited,' supporting the argument that ambiguous payment terms could be seen as an unfair practice. While the snippet focuses on entrepreneurial aspects, the change in payment obligation directly affects the compensation aspect of trade.

perturbation.original_text: Client will provide CRO with the documents and information necessary as requested by CRO for the conduct of the Services.
perturbation.changed_text: Client will attempt to provide CRO with documents and information for the conduct of the Services, as Client deems appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a legal obligation on the client to provide information that the CRO requests. Modifying the text to say 'attempt to provide' creates an ambiguity as to how much effort needs to be extended. Furthermore, adding 'as Client deems appropriate' gives the Client discretion on what information will be provided, which may not be enough to properly perform the Services.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR Part 312 - Investigational New Drug Application
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 312.23(a)(5) requires that an IND (Investigational New Drug) application include sufficient information to assure that clinical testing is safe. By modifying the client's requirement to provide the documents and information necessary for CRO to perform the clinical testing with 'as Client deems appropriate', this undermines the IND requirements, as the testing may not be able to be conducted safely due to lack of information.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the client's obligation to provide necessary documents, potentially undermining the safety of clinical testing. The original text requires the client to provide documents 'as requested by CRO,' whereas the modified text allows the client to provide documents 'as Client deems appropriate.' The Law Explanation states that 21 CFR § 312.23(a)(5) requires an IND application to include sufficient information to assure that clinical testing is safe. By allowing the client to determine what information is appropriate, the modified text may lead to insufficient information being provided, thereby contradicting the law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 21 CFR 312.23 which supports the regulatory context of the Law Explanation.
